sentences.text: Pascha is the one.
She used to deliver milk to our house every morning, you know.
She'd like-- the milkmans, they used to have-- you know, she used to bring milk.
So she was a very, very nice lady.
She gave me the package, and she gave me bread and milk.
And I felt better, you know.
But I never cried, never cried again.
And I sure went for survival.
Also, this was already been established the ghetto.
When we went the next day, you know, after they-- they took me out from the-- from the-- the death throes to save.
They took us out.
They took me out.
And we had to come to the-- when they established the ghetto and so on, so they-- they made us go.
They said we're going to go to Kozak.
That's the place where the killing was taking place.
I went there.
I mean, I had to go.
We came there.
Beautiful place in the forest.
And it's all empty.
Three massive graves.
Three massive graves.
And the people were laying one-- naked, one on top of the other.
And they gave them the bullets in the neck, apparently.
There was no blood, no blood whatsoever.
I don't know what kind of bullets they gave them.
And some of them were standing.
I remember one man was standing with his mouth wide open.
And we covered up.
They made us cover up.
It was so cruel.
They made us cover up the graves.
With earth.
With shovels.
Can you imagine such massive grave?
Five-- so many people.
Well, in the first one was at least 4,000.
Or 3 and 1/2 thousand.
And the first [NON-ENGLISH].
Because altogether was 5,000.
And that was another grave they made.
So we came-- then the second day when we came, you could see the earth is like moving up.
Apparently because there-- the blood was coming like, you know, on dry parched land.
You could see drenched with blood, you know, just like little rivers, like you know, that how you could see all that.
Three days we worked to cover up the graves.
I found my mother's scarf in there, the one she was wearing, down there.
I didn't cry.
I was stunned, you know.
You just-- it's-- it's unbelievable that they-- that such a thing could have happened.
I never saw that person in my life and here-- as a matter of fact, Leo's cousin in Israel crawled out from-- but he wasn't dead.
He-- he was-- he crawled out from one of them, you know.
And that how life with punishments started, you know.
[INAUDIBLE] That was Shavuot.
You know, that was in Erev Shavuot.
Must be around-- Shavuot is in May.
May, June, you know.
Then I used to go to the-- to the synagogue and say Kaddish morning and night.
Those days they allowed me, because I was-- you know, my brother was away.
He couldn't, so I would go.
And they would talk that there's going to be partisan.
But I was still young, so I wasn't allowed no place.
And everybody threw me around like I don't belong nowheres.
I'm-- I'm-- I'm an outcast, you know.
So it-- but-- but-- but from our town, you know, from my school, three of us were left, you know, alive [INAUDIBLE].
So we used to meet, the three of us together.
It was-- was [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and myself.
And we used to sit.
We cried, and we talked, and we-- what's going to happen and when it's going to happen, and so on and so forth.
And he was sad because his father committed suicide, you know, because he was-- then there was [?
Broder. ?] He became the head of the Judenrat.
And we're talking.
It's going to be another [NON-ENGLISH], they're going to be, which was Erev Sukkot.
And we just start [INAUDIBLE] in the ghetto.
They started to gather up in the ghetto on the next day, whatever.
They start to gather up people in the ghetto.
When they started to gather up people in the ghetto, the rabbi [INAUDIBLE] went out with the [INAUDIBLE] Torah, and he says, people, let's get in, get killed, and [NON-ENGLISH].
Where in the world can you run, OK?
But some people started to run.
And I started to run.
And I ran.
And down there we have a zamak.
Zamak, this is a-- the ruins from a castle was in our town.
And it's up a hill, and then you go down a hill, where it's the river, you know.
And I started to run.
Where I was running-- and-- but he burned the ghetto.
He put the ghetto on fire.
The rabbi and some people put the ghetto on fire.
And they started to run.
That's how we could run.
Erev Sukkot.
That was in 1942, were two [INAUDIBLE] you know, the [INAUDIBLE].
And I started to run, too.
I mean, I was running.
I was running and running and running.
I don't know where I was running, was running.
And if I took the-- looked around me, there was no one around.
I heard the-- the-- the-- the-- the shots, because they were up, you know, and we're down.
I saw-- I turned around and it's all I could see was [NON-ENGLISH] you know.
And she was [NON-ENGLISH].
She was laying face down and one arm in another direction, off-- her arm was torn off.
So she was dead, and I-- and I was running.
And I turned around and I was all by myself.
And that's the way I was running.
Don't ask me where I was going, why I was going, what I was doing, but I was running.
Oh, that-- that was in the-- in-- it was-- but, time-- it must have been around 11 o'clock or 12.
I don't know, but when I was running, I was running, I was running, it was evening.
That's all I know.
I don't know no food, no nothing.
I was just running.
I was running in the direction where my father-- towards [NON-ENGLISH].
Which direction did I know?

And I knew down there was a [NON-ENGLISH], a lady, and I remember that my-- my-- my father and mother, they gave away some stuff to-- to have it kept in there, in case, because to-- someday we'd be able to change for food [INAUDIBLE] with-- will-- when the war will end, we'll-- we'll have some stuff.
I came into her.
I found the place.
On the road, that's a story by itself, you know.
I-- I-- I got in with dogs.
And I-- I was near the policeman's, chief of the police house.
And there was something, I'm telling, a miracle.
Anyway, I landed in her house and she said to me, you know, there's a few Jewish people in that forest beside us.
So I-- I thanked her.
She gave me some food.
And I went into that forest.
That was [NON-ENGLISH], was [?
Shapiro. ?] There was quite a few people down there.
I dreamt, during the night, that my mother came to me.
And she says, why don't you go on the other side Sluch.
Down there's the [NON-ENGLISH].
Why don't you go down there, my child?
I get up in the morning, and I said, you know, you know what, that [INAUDIBLE] I was dreaming.
I never heard about them places.
Why don't we go?
Of course, I was a child.
They made fun of me.
So I says, you know what, it pulled me.
I'm going.
I stopped at [?
Yionka's ?] house, you know, and I says, you know, is there such a thing as [NON-ENGLISH].
She says, I don't know, but I says, it's on the other side of the Sluch, of the river, big river.
She said, but there's some-- there's [? police ?] on the other side of the Sluch.
I said, because I'm going.
She said, you know what, I'd be so happy if you'd go, because on account you Jewish people, they're liable to come and kill me too, and so on.
They came the same night and they killed all the Jews over there in that little forest.
And I was going.
So June [INAUDIBLE], I was going, and I hit a little village.
Not far, like in the back of the village, and there was a-- a cemetery.
I was so tired, I jumped into the hole.
There was-- I don't know how I crawled out, back.
Beats me.
I-- I went in, and I don't know how long I slept in there, and how long I've been down there.
And I didn't know they were going to bring a body, if they weren't going to bring a body.
To me, with my-- with my mind, as a child's mind, you know, with my mentality, I figured out I'd be saved.
And I jumped into that-- the six feet deep.
I had difficulties crawling out, but I did crawl out somehow.
And-- and I continued going.
And I got nourishment from-- from you know, in the fall, there's still some berries in there, you know.
Raw mushrooms I was eating, you know.
And then, when I passed the village, a little village, I used to go at night time and there were-- where they used to feed the pigs, you know, in the-- the trough-- trough.
I don't know what is it called.
I used to take out potatoes, all the-- so I kind of, you know, and the many times that I'd sleep in a-- where hay, you know in a-- in a-- in a [INAUDIBLE].
In a barn, till I came into-- to a little village over the Sluch, over the river down there.
To on the other side.
And it was [NON-ENGLISH].
Down there, I met a lady.
And I said, I come from [NON-ENGLISH], and I'm Ukrainian.
And I just don't-- you know, my parents, the Germans came, then they killed my parents, because, you know.
Oh, they took me in.
They took me in.
I spoke--
--you know, I spoke Ukrainian.
They were [NON-ENGLISH].
They didn't speak a good Polish, either.
And I said, what can you do?
I says, I'll do anything.
Anyway, I was knitting sweaters.
I learned how to weave the wool.
You have to-- you know.
And I was working there.
And then I was working as a-- took the-- the cows some pasture.
I used to take them out.
I worked in the fields.
I-- I did everything, you know.
Then they told me I can go to-- I should go to church with them.
So I went to church with them.
And then some Jewish people came into the-- in this forest.
A Mr.
Edelman ?] walked in-- in the house.
And he looks at me.
And he looks at me.
And he says, aren't you a Jewish girl?
Like this.
I said, no.
Anyway, but when I saw he-- he's a Jew, and he started to talk Yiddish, I was so happy, and I told him so.
So the lady right away told me, you can't stay here.
You have to go into the woods.
So I went.
And already I lost my position, the-- the high position which I had.
So, but at least I had food.
So I went into the woods, and at night time we used to go.
They knew that there are Jewish people in there.
But we used to go at nighttime and steal them blind from food-- with food.
Whatever they left outside hanging, nothing disappeared, because we didn't have no clothes [INAUDIBLE].
And then winter is coming, you know.
So winter time, it-- it-- it was hard, because they built-- a [NON-ENGLISH], it was called.
We dug ourselves in into the-- into the ground.
And you couldn't put a fire, because during the day, the smoke.
At nighttime, you would see the-- the-- the fire.
In the daytime, you'd see smoke.
So it was very, very hard.
But would you believe it, when the survival is there, you-- you want to survive, you'll survive in anything.
And then, somehow, God was with us, that the all village got sick on typhoid.
The typhoid.
Our body produces lice when it's the-- the circumstances like this.
I used to roll myself in the snow and the lice were crawling all over me.
I used to take on the fire and shake it out, and they cracked.
Can you imagine?
When they said we are not human, we're subhuman, that what they made out of us.
They did make out of us like this.
I was sick with typhoid.
Who diagnosed it?
Who knew?
But we knew.
Hair came out, the high fever.
You know, there was no water to drink, because it's [INAUDIBLE].
And you've got-- you-- you-- you took your shoes, what shoes we took from the-- from the tree, the bark of the tree, and we made-- you know--
--wrapped it.
And we took them, and that soaked [INAUDIBLE].
I used to suck it to drink.
The one time I urinated and drank it.
That how-- how bad it was, you know, but if you-- and I survived.
And in the village, they died.
They were just dying, funeral after funeral.
Then the Germans decided that the partisans are around the-- the forest, so they came and burned the villages.
Then the [INAUDIBLE] came to us.
At least we were already settled.
What do you know?
We already had our homes.
We had everything.
What do you know?
So we were hosting them.
And like this was going on, we knew the war is coming to an end.
How did you know that?
They-- the primitive ways are still the modern ways.
The modern technology couldn't do what the primitive ways were doing.
You put your ear to the ground, and you could hear there was shooting going on.
They went by the moon.
When the moon was red, they said that's a bloody war now going on.
They could tell.
But you know, we used to put every day, all the time lay with the-- with the ear to the ground and listen to it.
So we used to say, [NON-ENGLISH] is coming on the white horse, you know.
And then it was coming closer and closer and closer.
I could hear.
And then, one day, it walked into the village.
They walked in.
It's called a [NON-ENGLISH] that which they send out on the first, you know, to-- to-- to-- to-- to-- you know, they're sending out before the army comes in, or so on.
They-- they send out people you know, to-- to check it all out.
The Russian.
The-- the Red Army.
We saw them.
The-- the few soldiers.
In the beginning, was afraid to walk out.
But he says, don't be afraid, we're the Russian army.
And they gave us bread.
And they started to hug me.
Between them was a gypsy man.
I was bitten at one time by a dog, and he took out a piece of parachute and gave me a-- wrapped me around, and he gave me a hug.
It was-- it was hard to describe, you know.
I knew I have no home to go to.
I knew I'd have no place where to go.
But still, you know, you knew it's-- you were liberated.
You were free, even though we're afraid to walk out.
You know, it was-- it was like somebody is-- is over your shoulder-- somebody's-- is going to kill you any minute.
It's-- it's-- but the will to survive is stronger than anything, you know.
And that's how we were liberated.
So--
It was-- it was April or May.
I know it was started to get to spring, but it was still frozen.
The rivers were still frozen, because we get a late spring, you know.
And we started like the pilgrimage home.
We went back to our hometown.
Where else could we go?
Who knew the world?
I didn't know there was a world.
I come from a small town.
I didn't know what mean a red light or a green light.
I haven't-- you know, I didn't see a train.
I didn't see nothing.
I mean I-- I-- let's face it.
I'm a [NON-ENGLISH] But I had [NON-ENGLISH].
We had a good life.
So there we went.
We went back, all the people that was left.
There were quite a few people left.
And we started our journey.
We walked.
It was about 50 kilometer.
It was still cold, but we walked, blistered feet, and so on.
But by that time, they came out, the people.
They gave us food, you know.
We didn't have to steal, you know.
We can only say we're Jewish people.
And we come near the Sluch to go over the Sluch.
The Sluch isn't frozen, so we couldn't go.
So we had to go to Novograd Volynsk, another 30 kilometer around, walking the journey.
[INAUDIBLE]
There was no bridge to cross.
No, they-- they bombed the bridge.
And from-- and-- and the river wasn't frozen.
And from Novograd Volynsk, it's another 30 kilometer to our town.
And we came.
But what can I tell you?
From a town of 5 and 1/2 thousand people, we gathered by one table.
That's all was left us.
By one table, we gathered like one family, hugging one another.
And that's all was left.
And I was still an outcast, because this one had a brother.
This one had an uncle.
This one had an aunt.
This one-- I had no one.
But later I had a brother.
On the street.
He came in.
He came in.
I was walking, and he also came from-- he was hiding in Kiev on Polish papers.
He was in Kiev, and he also came into the town.
So then my life started to get better, you know.
But can you imagine from 5 and 1/2 thousand people to do such a Holocaust, such a tragedy?
To do it all in-- in two days, one day in the spring, and one day in the fall?
To murder out so many people?
I had to say I also had parents.
I also had relatives.
And you come out into this world.
You try to make a better place for your family.
You know what?
And you built this [INAUDIBLE] gave me so much hope.
And we built a new life.
We built a good life.
[PAUSES FOR 34 SECONDS]
In [NON-ENGLISH]?
I went between [NON-ENGLISH] and Kiev.
I was working at the beginning.
And then I went to school in Kiev.
So I went between [NON-ENGLISH] and Kiev, you know.
I was going, because when-- it's still my home.
It's still my roots, no matter what.
We couldn't go to Kazak.
We weren't allowed to go.
You know, when the Russia-- we were liberated.
We weren't allowed to go [NON-ENGLISH].
Yet I knew where it was.
It took 50 some years that I could go back, you know.
No, no the house wasn't there.
No.
Oh, that time?
With the Russia?
Yeah.
When we came, the house was there.
And a Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH] lived in there, you know.
But we didn't go.
We didn't stay in our house, no.
We stayed-- most of the people we gathered them on [NON-ENGLISH] Street, and we stayed over there.
And there was [NON-ENGLISH] at her place.
She used to make-- they made the [NON-ENGLISH].
And we gathered all into her place.
Look how many people that were there, you know.
She used to do cooking and baking.
And she says it's such a pleasure.
At least I've got, you know, but it-- it-- it was a-- like a family, you know.
We gathered like a family.
And-- but then later, I lived with my brother and my [? ex ?] sister-in-law, who lived further down, you know, in another part of the town.
Yeah, my brother was married.
And yeah, was married.
And as a matter of fact, my-- my sister-in-law was four months pregnant when he was killed.
My brother was killed after the war.
He was-- there's many stories were going around.
There was a train going from [NON-ENGLISH]-- from [NON-ENGLISH] to [NON-ENGLISH].
A train was going, and there were only two Jewish boys, [?
Goloup ?] and my brother.
And they were coming home, because it was [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know.
In Russia it's a big holiday.
And he was coming home, and I was coming from Kiev, from school I was coming home.
And he never arrived, and never arrived.
And then there were rumors around that they-- he was killed.
They bombed the train and so on.
Some people-- rumors were going around that the [NON-ENGLISH]-- you see, what-- [NON-ENGLISH].
What happened, they were promised independent Ukraine.
The-- the-- the German-- the Germans promised them independent Ukraine.
So they had-- they had guerrillas.
They had been [NON-ENGLISH].
They had [NON-ENGLISH].
And they--
Freedom fighters, you know.
And they were flushing them out.
The-- the Russians used to go on [NON-ENGLISH], used to call, you know, to-- to flush them out.
But anyway, they-- they-- they mined the train.
There were some people in that train.
I remember [?
Esmont. ?] And she came.
And she said that they-- they bombed the train.
She ran away.
She was in the woods.
And she survived and she came.
But only two.
So we figured out, they bombed the train and they-- the two were-- they-- they were killed.
Only about five years ago, I had a visitor from Montreal.
And he came to me.
And he says Fania, it's about time you should know the truth.
I said, what happened?
He says, they went because he wasn't there.
He was there.
And he said, we never wanted to tell you people, to [?
Sally ?] that the-- they went-- they came in, the [NON-ENGLISH], and they got two Jewish boys from the train, so point blank they came and shot them, point blank, because they were two Jewish boys.
So-- so that's my brother.
Yes, my brother.
One was in the woods with me, hiding, [?
Goloup. ?] And the other one which is a relative to my ex sister-in-law.
And the other one was my brother.
And my sister was four months pregnant.
When she was killed-- when he was killed.
Isn't that something, to live through the war and to-- and to-- to go through.
And a German made him papers.
You know, Polish papers.
So they were in Kiev.
He was, you know, was-- was working very strong for the underground, you know, for the Russian, for the partisan.
To me?
I was-- I was in Kiev, you know.
I was going to school.
You wouldn't believe it.
[NON-ENGLISH], you know, [NON-ENGLISH].
But we were going then, that time, with [INAUDIBLE], with our own generators and so on.
I went with [INAUDIBLE].
She's now in Israel, so the two of us.
And we were good students, so we-- we didn't have to pay, we got [NON-ENGLISH].
So yes.
And the-- the weekends we had to work on the [NON-ENGLISH] because it was so badly bombed.
So we worked with the prisoner of wars, together.
But they were under the guard and we were-- we were cleaning up.
So now-- now when I was in Kiev, I says, I worked in there.
And then the school, they used to send us-- weekends, we had to take their-- their-- their uniforms, go the Dnieper, and wash it in cold water.
They gave us a bunch of uniforms.
And bring it back.
That was our days off.
And summer, we had to go and work on the [NON-ENGLISH], because you know, for our-- that time, I just thought that we were-- they did us any harm, it's not right.
But when I see it now, I mean, they-- listen, we had to pay somehow for our teaching.
And that was, I think, a six months course, whatever.
And then they kept sending us from place to place to project.
Oh, most of them were the movies from the war, most of them.
We showed one which is a-- a classic for-- with [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the-- the-- the movie star.
Paul Muni, you know, you know.
Black and white.
And every time it used to tear in the middle, so they used to scream at us and so on.
That how I met Leo's father.
I was working.
I was projecting moves in the-- their town.
And I also worked as a-- worked in a restaurant, you know.
My husband.
That where I met my husband's father.
It's a-- it was a-- it wasn't a easy life, let me tell you.
I was all by myself, and I had to-- had to-- you know, I used to come home and help out my sister-in-law, and so on.
So it wasn't a very easy life, believe me.
Then in the middle she-- she had a brother, and he was [NON-ENGLISH].
She says, do me a favor.
Go and find my brother.
So-- so I go to [NON-ENGLISH], and down there.
Six weeks, I was on the trains, winter time, to find her brother.
While I was going to go to look for him there, he was there.
It was-- and you couldn't-- those days, was no such a thing as a ticket.
You just rode the trains, you know, hanging on, with outside.
Look, I had a very, very hard-- it wasn't-- wasn't easy, even though I was liberated.
But you know, when I was liberated, I was afraid for a long time.
When somebody knocked on the door, I used to go under the bed.
For a while, I couldn't sleep on a bed.
I was embarrassed, you know, when I came in.
So I used to go to bed and then at night I'm going go and lay on the floor.
Because I wasn't used to it.
I- it-- I was hurting to sleep in a bed, you see.
So there where I used to go, go on the floor.
And when somebody came in and-- oh, took me a long time.
I always looked over the shoulders.
I was very, very afraid.
I was frightened, you know.
But, of course, I didn't cry.
But I was very-- you know, it-- it's like a weight was all my life on the chest.
And it's now, when it comes Erev-- Erev Shavuot, I have it the same way.
I don't need a calendar to remind me or so on.
I just-- it's a weight.
It's over there [INAUDIBLE], you know.
No matter what, it's-- you cannot-- you are so scarred inside, that it's very hard to-- to-- to explain the feeling that you carry with you.
It's very-- we also ruin our kids this way, we hang on so much, because we lost so much, you know, that we hang on to them.
I know I did.
You know, they're ready to leave home, because you hang on.
Don't go here.
And don't go there.
You will get hurt, you'll-- you know, but afraid to lose what we have, because what-- what do we have now except the children?
They're our life.
Thank you.
It's the end of tape.
